You said:
In addition to Catastrophic Forgetting there is also the issue of Context Rot.  We need a system that learns preferences, new facts, feedback from repeated avoidable mistakes, avoids forgetting, avoids hallucinations, optimizes context use, is efficient.

ChatGPT said:
Now we’re talking about the real problem.

You don’t just want “learning.”
You want a system that:

Learns new facts

Learns preferences

Learns from repeated mistakes

Doesn’t forget old capabilities

Doesn’t hallucinate

Doesn’t let context decay into mush

Uses compute efficiently

That’s not one technique. That’s a systems architecture problem.

Let’s break this down cleanly and build a conceptual model you can use in your explainer.

Two Different Failure Modes: Forgetting vs Context Rot
These get conflated constantly, but they are fundamentally different.

1️⃣ Catastrophic Forgetting
Weight-space problem.

When you update parameters, you overwrite previous capabilities.

This happens during:

Fine-tuning

Continual training

Online updates

It’s a plasticity vs stability conflict.

2️⃣ Context Rot
Inference-time problem.

Context rot happens when:

The context window grows large

Important signals get diluted

Earlier instructions are forgotten or distorted

The model starts “pattern averaging” instead of reasoning

It is not forgetting weights.
It is attention dilution + distribution drift inside the prompt.

Think of it like this:

Catastrophic forgetting = brain damage
Context rot = short-term memory overload

Different layer of the stack.

The System You’re Describing Is Layered
You want five capabilities:

Persistent preference learning

Persistent fact learning

Mistake correction

No forgetting

Efficient context use

These live in different architectural layers.

Let’s structure them.

Layer 1 — Core Brain (Pretraining + Stable Fine-Tuning)
Goal:

General competence

Stable foundation

Risk:

Forgetting

Tools:

Replay methods

Subspace regularization (ELLA-type approaches)

Shared LoRA spaces

Periodic consolidation instead of constant updates

This layer changes rarely.

Layer 2 — Modular Skill Updates (Adapters / PEFT)
Goal:

Add skills without rewriting brain

Versionable specialization

Benefits:

Reversible

Mergeable

Lower forgetting risk

This is where “continuous improvement” often belongs.

Layer 3 — External Memory (RAG / Engram / CAG)
Goal:

Learn new facts without touching weights

Persist across restarts

Avoid forgetting entirely

Key insight:
Most “learning new facts” should not modify weights.

Weights are for generalization.
Memory is for storage.

If you store everything in weights, you create interference.

If you store everything in memory, you avoid forgetting but increase retrieval complexity.

The art is balancing both.

Layer 4 — Context Management (Preventing Context Rot)
Now we’re in RLM territory.

Context rot is fixed by:

Structured context segmentation

Retrieval prioritization

Recursive reasoning passes

Explicit memory indexing

Context compression/summarization

KV-cache-aware pruning

Task decomposition

Recursive Language Models shine here because they:

Re-query memory instead of dragging everything forward

Re-ground each reasoning step

Reconstruct context per call instead of inheriting decay

They treat context as a dynamic environment, not a static blob.

That’s a huge conceptual shift.

Layer 5 — Error Capture & Feedback Loop
Now we get to real “continuous learning.”

Agents produce:

Prompts

Tool calls

Outputs

Failures

Corrections

User preferences

These logs are gold.

A proper system:

Captures structured traces

Scores them (unit tests, static analysis, preference signals)

Clusters recurring failure patterns

Periodically trains lightweight updates

Evaluates retention before merging

This prevents:

Repeated avoidable mistakes

Silent regressions

This is not real-time learning.
It’s periodic consolidation.

Human analogy:
Sleep.

Hallucination Control Is Separate
Hallucination is not forgetting and not context rot.

It is:

Confidence without grounding

Pattern completion without verification

Solutions live in:

Retrieval grounding (RAG/Engram)

Tool verification

Self-consistency passes

Multi-step decomposition

Uncertainty estimation

Constrained decoding

External validators

Hallucination decreases when:

The model can check things.

The model doesn’t rely solely on internal parametric memory.

Continuous Learning Is Not One Thing
For your explainer, this framing works well:

Continuous learning is a coordination problem across time scales.

Time Scale	Mechanism	What It Fixes
Seconds	RLM / better context	Context rot
Minutes	Retrieval / memory updates	New facts
Days	Adapter updates	New skills
Weeks	Consolidated fine-tune	Integration
Months	New pretraining	Capability shifts
Most systems should:

Update memory frequently

Update adapters occasionally

Update core weights rarely

Efficiency Dimension
You also mentioned efficiency. That matters.

Efficiency improvements come from:

Avoiding giant context blobs (RLM, Engram-style lookup)

Caching structured intermediate outputs (CAG)

Adapter swapping instead of model cloning

Evaluating only changed capabilities

Distillation to compress improvements

Continuous learning without efficiency becomes financially impossible.

The Core Tension
You want:

Plasticity (learn new stuff fast)
Stability (don’t forget)
Groundedness (don’t hallucinate)
Efficiency (don’t burn GPUs)
Context sanity (don’t rot)

These are competing objectives.

Modern research is trying to:

Separate memory from weights

Separate reasoning from storage

Separate training from deployment

Separate short-term adaptation from long-term consolidation

831

855	You want a structured, multi-part blog series that:
856	
857	Explains all the major learning mechanisms in modern LLMs and agents
858	
859	Separates catastrophic forgetting from context rot
860	
861	Connects memory, adapters, RAG, Engram, RLM, and continual learning

864	
865	Still respects developers and researchers
866	
867	So we design this like a clean system architecture: layered, progressive, cumulative.
868	
869	Below is a proposed series plan, followed by drafted posts.
870	
871	📚 Series Title
872	“How AI Learns (and Forgets): Building Continuous Learning Systems for LLMs and Agents”
873	
874	🧭 Series Structure
875	Part 1 — The Many Meanings of “Learning”

896	Now I’ll draft these in digestible form. Each part ~900–1200 words, structured, readable.
897	
898	🧠 Part 1 — The Many Meanings of “Learning”
899	When people say, “AI learned something,” they usually mean one of at least four very different things.
900	
901	Large Language Models (LLMs) do not learn in one single way. They learn at different time scales, in different locations, and with very different consequences.
902	
903	To understand modern AI systems — especially agents — we need to separate these layers.
904	
905	Four Time Scales of Learning
906	1. Pretraining (Years)
907	This is the foundation.
908	
909	The model trains on massive datasets using gradient descent. The result is a set of weights — billions of parameters — encoding statistical structure of language and knowledge.
910	
911	This learning:
912	
913	Is slow and expensive
914	
915	Persists across restarts
916	
917	Cannot easily be reversed
918	
919	Is vulnerable to interference if modified later
920	
921	Think of this as long-term biological memory.
922	
923	2. Fine-Tuning (Days to Weeks)
924	Fine-tuning modifies the weights further, but with narrower data.
925	
926	This includes:
927	
928	Instruction tuning
929	
930	Alignment methods (RLHF, DPO)
931	
932	Domain adaptation
933	
934	Parameter-efficient methods like LoRA
935	
936	This is still weight-based learning.
937	
938	It persists across restarts.
939	It risks catastrophic forgetting.
940	It modifies the brain itself.
941	
942	3. Memory-Based Learning (Seconds to Minutes)
943	This is where many modern systems shift.
944	
945	Instead of changing weights, they store information externally:
946	
947	RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
948	
949	CAG (Cache-Augmented Generation)
950	
951	Vector databases
952	
953	Engram-style memory modules
954	
955	The model retrieves relevant memory per query.
956	
957	The brain stays stable. The notebook grows.
958	
959	This learning:
960	
961	Persists across restarts
962	
963	Survives model upgrades
964	
965	Does not cause forgetting
966	
967	Is fast
968	
969	4. In-Context Learning (Milliseconds)
970	This is temporary reasoning scaffolding.
971	
972	Information exists only in the prompt window.
973	
974	It:
975	
976	Does not update weights
977	
978	Does not persist across sessions
979	
980	Is powerful but fragile
981	
982	Suffers from context rot
983	
984	This is working memory.
985	
986	Why This Matters
987	Most discussions collapse all of this into “the model learned.”
988	
989	But:
990	
991	Updating weights risks forgetting.
992	
993	Updating memory does not.
994

file.content:
That separation is the big trend.

A Clean Conceptual Diagram For Your Video
Imagine four concentric rings:

Center: Core Weights (slow-changing)
Next: Adapters (modular learning)
Next: External Memory (facts & logs)
Outer: Context Manager (RLM + retrieval)

Then show arrows:

Errors feed into memory

Memory feeds into retrieval

Retrieval feeds into context

Periodic consolidation updates adapters

Rare consolidation updates core weights

That visual alone would make the explainer powerful.

The Big Idea To Emphasize
Continuous learning is not about constantly updating the brain.

It’s about:

Storing experiences safely

Using them intelligently

Consolidating selectively

Preserving stability

Most “AI that learns” hype collapses these layers into one idea.

Your video can separate them cleanly.

If you want next, we can:

Build a storyboard outline (10–15 minute explainer format),

Or create a comparative table slide pack,

Or design a conceptual Rust-based agent architecture that embodies these principles.


You said:
Start with a (multi-part series) blog post(s) that cover all of this in structured digestible parts.  What is the plan for such a series of posts.  Can you draft the posts.

ChatGPT said:
You want a structured, multi-part blog series that:

Explains all the major learning mechanisms in modern LLMs and agents

Separates catastrophic forgetting from context rot

Connects memory, adapters, RAG, Engram, RLM, and continual learning

Stays digestible for general readers

Still respects developers and researchers

So we design this like a clean system architecture: layered, progressive, cumulative.

Below is a proposed series plan, followed by drafted posts.

📚 Series Title
“How AI Learns (and Forgets): Building Continuous Learning Systems for LLMs and Agents”

🧭 Series Structure
Part 1 — The Many Meanings of “Learning”
Time scales of learning. Weights vs memory vs context.

Part 2 — Catastrophic Forgetting vs Context Rot
Two different failure modes. Different layers. Different fixes.

Part 3 — Weight-Based Learning
Pretraining, fine-tuning, LoRA, alignment, distillation.

Part 4 — Memory-Based Learning
RAG, CAG, Engram, model editing, grounding.

Part 5 — Context Engineering & Recursive Reasoning
ICL, RLM, structured context, tool use, preventing rot.

Part 6 — Toward Continuous Learning
Replay, subspace methods, adapter evolution, consolidation loops.

Part 7 — Designing a Practical Continuous Learning Agent
System architecture. Logging, feedback loops, evaluation, safety.

Now I’ll draft these in digestible form. Each part ~900–1200 words, structured, readable.

🧠 Part 1 — The Many Meanings of “Learning”
When people say, “AI learned something,” they usually mean one of at least four very different things.

Large Language Models (LLMs) do not learn in one single way. They learn at different time scales, in different locations, and with very different consequences.

To understand modern AI systems — especially agents — we need to separate these layers.

Four Time Scales of Learning
1. Pretraining (Years)
This is the foundation.

The model trains on massive datasets using gradient descent. The result is a set of weights — billions of parameters — encoding statistical structure of language and knowledge.

This learning:

Is slow and expensive

Persists across restarts

Cannot easily be reversed

Is vulnerable to interference if modified later

Think of this as long-term biological memory.

2. Fine-Tuning (Days to Weeks)
Fine-tuning modifies the weights further, but with narrower data.

This includes:

Instruction tuning

Alignment methods (RLHF, DPO)

Domain adaptation

Parameter-efficient methods like LoRA

This is still weight-based learning.

It persists across restarts.
It risks catastrophic forgetting.
It modifies the brain itself.

3. Memory-Based Learning (Seconds to Minutes)
This is where many modern systems shift.

Instead of changing weights, they store information externally:

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)

CAG (Cache-Augmented Generation)

Vector databases

Engram-style memory modules

The model retrieves relevant memory per query.

The brain stays stable. The notebook grows.

This learning:

Persists across restarts

Survives model upgrades

Does not cause forgetting

Is fast

4. In-Context Learning (Milliseconds)
This is temporary reasoning scaffolding.

Information exists only in the prompt window.

It:

Does not update weights

Does not persist across sessions

Is powerful but fragile

Suffers from context rot

This is working memory.

Why This Matters
Most discussions collapse all of this into “the model learned.”

But:

Updating weights risks forgetting.

Updating memory does not.

Updating prompts does not persist.

Updating adapters can be modular and reversible.

Continuous learning systems must coordinate all four.

In Part 2, we’ll examine the two fundamental failure modes that arise from confusing these layers: catastrophic forgetting and context rot.

🔥 Part 2 — Catastrophic Forgetting vs Context Rot
There are two fundamentally different failure modes in modern AI systems.

They are often confused. They should not be.

Catastrophic Forgetting (Weight-Space Failure)
When you fine-tune a model on new tasks, performance on older tasks may degrade.

This happens because gradient descent updates overlap in parameter space.

The model does not “know” which weights correspond to which task. It optimizes globally.

Solutions include:

Replay (training on old + new data)

Subspace regularization

Shared adapter spaces

Freezing base weights

Parameter-efficient methods

This is a stability vs plasticity tradeoff.

Context Rot (Inference-Time Failure)
Context rot is not weight damage.

It happens when:

Prompts grow too large

Earlier instructions get diluted

Attention spreads thin

The model begins averaging patterns instead of reasoning

Context rot is short-term memory decay.

It is solved by:

Retrieval-based context selection

Recursive reasoning passes

Structured tool calls

Summarization and compression

Memory indexing

Recursive Language Models (RLMs) are one attempt to address this problem by treating context as an environment that can be re-queried rather than dragged forward.

Understanding these two failures clarifies everything that follows.

In Part 3, we’ll examine weight-based learning in detail.

🏗 Part 3 — Weight-Based Learning
Weight-based learning modifies the neural network itself.

It is slow. It is powerful. It is dangerous.

Pretraining
This creates the base model.

It encodes language structure, reasoning patterns, and general world knowledge.

It is rarely repeated for cost reasons.

Fine-Tuning
Fine-tuning adapts models for specific tasks.

Examples:

Instruction following

Code generation

Medical domain knowledge

Risk:

Catastrophic forgetting

Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (LoRA and Adapters)
LoRA inserts small low-rank matrices into transformer layers.

Instead of changing all weights, it trains these small matrices.

Benefits:

Faster training

Modular updates

Lower forgetting risk

Version control

Shared adapter subspaces extend this idea by reducing interference between tasks.

Distillation
A larger model teaches a smaller one.

Distillation compresses capability into fewer parameters.

It:

Improves efficiency

Often improves stability

Does not solve forgetting alone

Weight-based learning is slow to change but forms the stable core.

In Part 4, we explore learning without changing weights at all.

📚 Part 4 — Memory-Based Learning
Modern AI systems increasingly rely on external memory.

This shifts “learning” away from parameters.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
Documents are embedded into vectors.

At query time:

Relevant vectors are retrieved

Injected into prompt

Used to ground responses

The model does not need to remember facts internally.

Cache-Augmented Generation (CAG)
Repeated contexts or key-value states are cached.

This avoids recomputation.

Engram-Style Memory
Recent proposals introduce conditional memory modules with direct indexing.

Instead of scanning large context, the system performs constant-time lookup.

The goal:
Separate static knowledge from dynamic reasoning.

Memory-based systems:

Avoid catastrophic forgetting

Persist across restarts

Scale independently of model weights

They introduce new engineering challenges:

Retrieval precision

Latency

Index maintenance

In Part 5, we examine context engineering and recursive reasoning.

🔁 Part 5 — Context Engineering & Recursive Reasoning
Large context windows are not a complete solution.

As context grows:

Attention dilutes

Errors compound

Reasoning quality degrades

In-Context Learning (ICL)
The model adapts temporarily via prompt examples.

Powerful but ephemeral.

Recursive Language Models (RLM)
RLMs decompose reasoning into multiple passes.

Instead of dragging entire context forward:

Re-query environment

Retrieve structured memory

Execute tools

Reconstruct context per step

file.content:
This prevents context rot by rebuilding focus each cycle.

Agents increasingly use:

Tool calls

Code execution

Search

Structured planning

Context becomes dynamic, not static.

In Part 6, we connect all this to continuous learning.

♻️ Part 6 — Toward Continuous Learning
Continuous learning aims to:

Learn new skills

Retain old skills

Avoid retraining from scratch

Avoid catastrophic forgetting

Approaches include:

Replay-based training

Replay-free regularization

Shared evolving adapter subspaces

Test-time adaptation

Periodic consolidation

A practical pattern emerges:

Log interactions

Score outcomes

Cluster failure types

Train lightweight updates

Evaluate retention

Deploy modular updates

Learning becomes cyclical, not constant.

In the final part, we design a full system.

🧩 Part 7 — Designing a Continuous Learning Agent
A robust architecture contains:

Core model (rarely updated)
+
Adapters (modular skills)
+
External memory (facts)
+
Context manager (RLM-style)
+
Logging & evaluation loop

Errors feed into memory first.

Only recurring, validated improvements reach adapters.

Only major, stable shifts reach base weights.

This layered approach:

Reduces forgetting

Reduces hallucination

Reduces context rot

Improves efficiency

Enables safe improvement over time

Closing Theme for the Series
AI does not learn in one place.

It learns in layers:

Permanent

Modular

External

Temporary

Continuous learning is not constant weight updates.

It is careful coordination across time scales.

If you’d like next, we can:

Refine these into publication-ready Markdown with diagrams

Add citations to specific recent papers

Or convert this into a companion YouTube explainer outline with visual slide plans


You said:
We need diagrams, illustrations for the various concepts.  They should be in the style of PaperBanana (q.v. online for examples)

ChatGPT said:
Excellent. If we’re going “PaperBanana style,” we want:

Flat, minimalist vector diagrams

Soft pastel blocks

Clean arrows

Rounded rectangles

Minimal text inside shapes

White background

Sparse but intentional color

Concept-first layout

Think: academic figure, but friendly.

Below is a diagram plan for the series, with illustration concepts and ready-to-generate prompts. I’ll structure this as a visual blueprint for each blog part.

🎨 Visual Language for the Series
Before the individual diagrams, define a consistent design system:

Palette

Core Weights: muted blue

Adapters: soft orange

Memory: pastel green

Context/Prompt: lavender

Tools/Environment: light gray

Error/Feedback loop: soft red arrows

Shapes

Weights: stacked rounded blocks

Memory: database cylinder or grid

Context: transparent layered sheets

Adapters: small plug-in rectangles

RLM recursion: circular arrows

Feedback loop: dashed arrows

This consistency makes the entire series feel coherent.

Part 1 Diagram — “Four Time Scales of Learning”
Concept
Concentric rings radiating outward from core weights.


https://www.infodiagram.com/api/img/f%3Apng/w%3A3072/aHR0cHM6Ly9zdG9yYWdlLmdvb2dsZWFwaXMuY29tL2luZm9kaWFncmFtLmFwcHNwb3QuY29tL3N0cmFwaS9nbG9iZV9sYXllcnNfY2Y2ZjE1OGRmNS9nbG9iZV9sYXllcnNfY2Y2ZjE1OGRmNS5wbmc%3D

https://visionbook.mit.edu/figures/neural_nets/MLP1.png

https://learnarchitecture.online/cdn/shop/files/cXeNEspYE7.png?v=1727634435
4
Layout
Center circle:
Core Weights (Pretraining) – blue

Next ring:
Fine-tuning / Adapters – orange segments

Next ring:
External Memory (RAG / Engram) – green segmented ring

Outer translucent layer:
Prompt / In-Context Learning – lavender haze

Caption:
“Learning happens at different layers and time scales.”

Part 2 Diagram — Catastrophic Forgetting vs Context Rot
Concept
Split-panel comparison.


https://www.researchgate.net/publication/359390774/figure/fig1/AS%3A1136271070101505%401647919542799/An-illustration-of-the-catastrophic-forgetting-problem-and-importance-of-the-relation.jpg

“Weight-space interference”

Attention arrows thinning

“Attention dilution in long context”

Part 3 Diagram — Weight-Based Learning

Small orange plug-in blocks labeled “LoRA”

Arrow showing modular insertion

Part 4 Diagram — Memory-Based Learning
Concept
Brain vs Notebook metaphor.


https://images.vexels.com/media/users/3/224508/raw/1b93c2c96330a0a7109efbefe2166053-brain-circuit-illustration-design.jpg

4
Diagram Layout
Left:

Right:

Arrows:
Query → Retrieve → Inject into Context

Second figure:
Indexed memory slots with direct arrow (Engram concept)

Caption:
“Store facts outside the brain.”

Part 5 Diagram — Recursive Language Model (RLM)
Concept
Context as environment.


https://www.infodiagram.com/api/img/f%3Apng/w%3A3072/aHR0cHM6Ly9zdG9yYWdlLmdvb2dsZWFwaXMuY29tL2luZm9kaWFncmFtLmFwcHNwb3QuY29tL3N0cmFwaS9jeWNsZV9sb29wX2RpYWdyYW1fM2Q0MjM1ZTg4OC9jeWNsZV9sb29wX2RpYWdyYW1fM2Q0MjM1ZTg4OC5wbmc%3D

Part 6 Diagram — Continuous Learning Loop

Optional Advanced Visuals for Later Posts

content.text:
I read the full file. Important note: **the file does not contain 135 distinct ML topics.** It's a 7-part blog series discussion covering roughly 40 named ML concepts (pre-training, LoRA, RAG, CAG, Engram, ICL, RLM, ELLA, Share, FOREVER, DPO, etc.). I'll work from what's actually there. If you have a separate list of 135, point me at it.

## Topics in the file (~40)

**Weight-space learning:** pre-training, full fine-tuning, instruction tuning/SFT, RLHF, RLAIF, DPO, online preference, distillation, model editing, test-time learning

**PEFT family:** LoRA, shared LoRA, prompt-tuning, prefix-tuning, adapters, IA³, PaCT, ELLA (subspace regularization), Share (shared evolving subspaces), FOREVER (model-centric replay timing)

**Memory/retrieval:** RAG, CAG, KV-cache pruning, vector DBs, embeddings, Engram (O(1) lookup)

**Inference-time:** ICL, RLM (recursive language models), tool use, self-consistency, constrained decoding, uncertainty estimation, multi-step decomposition

**System concepts:** catastrophic forgetting, context rot, stability/plasticity, hallucination grounding, replay buffers, canary deployments, retention tests

## Demo potential in MLPL today

### 5 topics demoable NOW with minimal additions

| # | Topic | What MLPL needs |
|---|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Linear regression** (gradient descent for regression) | Nothing — just remove sigmoid from existing demo |
| 2 | **Softmax classification** (multi-class) | `softmax`, `argmax` built-ins (~50 LOC) |
| 3 | **K-means clustering** (1D/2D toy) | `argmin`, basic indexing slice (`a[i]`), `where` (~150 LOC) |
| 4 | **Cosine similarity / nearest neighbor** (toy embeddings) | `norm` built-in, broadcasting div across rows (~80 LOC) |
| 5 | **Loss curve over training** | Just collect a vector during `repeat`, no new features needed for the data (visualization needs SVG) |

These are the **easiest 5 because the math is just matmul + element-wise + reductions**, all of which exist.

### With SVG viz: many more become demoable

The `svg(data, type)` proposal is the right move. Suggested signature:

```
svg(data, "scatter")        # 2D points
svg(data, "line")           # series
svg(data, "bar")            # histogram
svg(matrix, "heatmap")      # 2D values → color
svg(matrix, "contour")      # loss landscape
svg(points, "scatter3d")    # 2D projection of 3D
svg(graph, "network")       # nodes/edges
svg(matrix, "decision_boundary")  # classifier output over grid
```

Returns a string. The REPL detects `<svg>` prefix and renders it inline.

**Topics unlocked with SVG:**
- Gradient descent loss curve (line plot)
- Decision boundary for logistic/softmax (heatmap + scatter)
- K-means clusters (scatter w/ color)
- PCA projection (scatter)
- Embeddings 2D (scatter w/ labels)
- Attention weight matrix (heatmap)
- Confusion matrix (heatmap)
- Stability vs plasticity tradeoff curve (line)
- Loss landscape contour (illustrating local minima)
- Catastrophic forgetting demo (line plot showing old-task accuracy degrading as new task is learned — needs sequential training, all primitives exist)

That's ~10 topics from the blog series that become **directly demoable** once SVG ships, plus the ~5 above without viz.

## Topics that need substantial work

| Topic | Needs |
|-------|-------|
| LoRA / PEFT | Real tensor backend (MLX or CUDA), gradient tape, transformer arch |
| Distillation | Teacher/student models, real LLM hosting |
| RAG | HTTP client + embedding model + vector store |
| CAG | KV-cache abstraction (not meaningful in MLPL alone) |
| Engram | Hash-indexed memory primitive, easy to prototype |
| RLM | Tool-use loop, structured prompts, LLM client |
| RLHF/DPO | Real LLM, preference data pipeline |
| ELLA / Share / FOREVER | LoRA infra first, then specific techniques |

## Proposed sagas and milestones

Each saga is ~6–10 steps. Milestones group sagas into shippable releases.

### Milestone v0.3 — Visualization & Classical ML

**Saga: mlpl-svg-viz** (foundation for everything visual)
1. `svg()` built-in skeleton + scatter type
2. Line plot type (loss curves)
3. Bar / histogram type
4. Heatmap type (2D matrix → color)
5. REPL inline SVG rendering (web app sniffs `<svg`, renders in output)
6. Decision boundary helper (eval classifier over grid)
7. Doc + demos page

**Saga: mlpl-ml-primitives**
1. `softmax`, `argmax`, `argmin`
2. `relu`, `leaky_relu`
3. `cross_entropy`, `mse_loss`
4. Array slicing `a[i]` and `a[i:j]`
5. `norm`, `normalize`
6. `where` / boolean masking
7. Random number generation (seedable)

**Saga: mlpl-classical-ml-demos**
1. Linear regression demo (with loss-curve viz)
2. Softmax classifier (decision boundary viz)
3. K-means clustering (animated steps)
4. PCA via power iteration (2D projection viz)
5. kNN (nearest neighbor viz)
6. Catastrophic forgetting toy demo (sequential 2-task learning, line plot of both accuracies)

→ **Ships v0.3:** browser REPL can teach the entire classical ML curriculum visually

---

### Milestone v0.4 — LLM Client

**Saga: mlpl-http-json**
1. `mlpl-net` crate with HTTP client (reqwest, native-only — not in WASM build)
2. JSON literal syntax in MLPL or `json()` built-in
3. `http_post(url, body)` returning string
4. Error handling for network failures

**Saga: mlpl-llm-client**
1. Provider abstraction: `Provider` trait (Ollama, Anthropic, OpenAI)
2. `llm_complete(prompt, opts)` built-in
3. `llm_embed(text)` returning vector
4. Streaming responses (optional)
5. Server binary (`mlpl-server`) that proxies LLM calls so the WASM REPL can use them
6. Demo: chat loop in MLPL

→ **Ships v0.4:** call Ollama/Claude/GPT from MLPL, embed text, get vectors

---

### Milestone v0.5 — RAG, Engram, Memory Primitives

**Saga: mlpl-vector-store**
1. In-memory vector store: `vector_store_new()`, `vector_store_add(store, key, vec)`, `vector_store_search(store, query, k)`
2. Cosine similarity ranking
3. Persistence to disk (JSON or binary)
4. Demo: tiny RAG pipeline (embed corpus, query, retrieve, prompt LLM)

**Saga: mlpl-engram**
1. Hash-indexed memory: `engram_new()`, `engram_set(key, value)`, `engram_get(key)`
2. Composite-key support
3. Demo: O(1) lookup vs RAG comparison with timing visualization

→ **Ships v0.5:** can demo RAG, Engram, and the "memory vs weights" distinction interactively

---

### Milestone v0.6 — Hardware Backend (MLX / CUDA)

**Saga: mlpl-tensor-backend-trait**
1. `Tensor` trait abstracting backend
2. CPU implementation (existing DenseArray)
3. Backend selection at session creation

**Saga: mlpl-mlx-bridge** (Apple Silicon)
1. FFI to MLX C API
2. Tensor allocation/copy
3. matmul, element-wise ops on MLX
4. Autograd tape (forward + backward for matmul, sigmoid, mse)
5. Benchmark vs CPU

**Saga: mlpl-cuda-bridge** (Nvidia, parallel to MLX)
1. cuBLAS FFI for matmul
2. Custom kernels for activations
3. Same autograd surface as MLX bridge

→ **Ships v0.6:** real tensor ops on GPU/Neural Engine, ready for fine-tuning

---

### Milestone v0.7 — LoRA Fine-tuning

**Saga: mlpl-lora**
1. LoRA layer primitive: low-rank A·B matrices
2. Apply LoRA to a linear layer
3. Training loop with autograd
4. Save/load LoRA weights
5. Merge LoRA into base
6. Demo: fine-tune a tiny transformer block on a toy task with loss-curve viz
7. Multi-LoRA: swap adapters at inference

**Saga: mlpl-shared-subspace** (ELLA / Share-style)
1. Shared low-rank subspace across tasks
2. Demo: train task A, then task B, show stability/plasticity curve
3. Compare to vanilla LoRA on same tasks

→ **Ships v0.7:** real LoRA fine-tuning demos, with viz of forgetting

---

### Milestone v0.8 — Continuous Learning Loop

**Saga: mlpl-trace-logging**
1. Structured trace export (already partially done)
2. Trace replay
3. Trace clustering by failure mode

**Saga: mlpl-eval-harness**
1. Task suite definition
2. Retention test runner
3. Forward-transfer test runner
4. Regression detection

**Saga: mlpl-continuous-loop**
1. Periodic consolidation: collect → score → train LoRA → eval → deploy
2. Rollback on regression
3. Demo: agent learns from mistakes over a day

→ **Ships v0.8:** end-to-end continuous-learning demo loop

---

### Milestone v0.9 — RLM and Agent Loop

**Saga: mlpl-tool-use**
1. Tool registration: `register_tool("calc", fn)`
2. LLM tool-call parsing
3. Tool execution in MLPL
4. Demo: agent uses MLPL itself as a calculator tool

**Saga: mlpl-rlm**
1. Recursive context environment
2. Re-query patterns
3. Context compression
4. Demo: long-document QA with RLM vs naive long-context

→ **Ships v0.9:** the full agent stack the blog series describes

## Architecture additions implied

```
mlpl-net          (HTTP, only in native build)
mlpl-llm          (provider trait + impls)
mlpl-vector       (vector store)
mlpl-tensor       (backend trait)
mlpl-mlx          (Apple backend)
mlpl-cuda         (Nvidia backend)
mlpl-lora         (LoRA primitives)
mlpl-server       (proxy for browser REPL → LLMs)
```

Browser REPL stays valuable for v0.3/v0.5 (no network needed for vector store + svg). For v0.4+ that touches LLMs/GPUs, the demos run in the native REPL (or via the mlpl-server proxy from the browser).

## Recommendation on what to do first

The **highest-leverage next saga is `mlpl-svg-viz`** because:
- It enables ~10 topics from the blog series immediately
- It's pure additive (no architecture changes)
- It works in the browser REPL right now
- It's the visual "wow" that makes the language teachable
- The blog series explicitly asks for diagrams in PaperBanana style — MLPL could *generate* them

Want me to archive the current saga, plan `mlpl-svg-viz` in detail, and stage it as the next saga to run?

input.content:
# MLPL SVG Visualization Saga

## Quality Requirements (apply to EVERY step)

Every step MUST:
1. Follow TDD: write failing tests FIRST, then implement, then refactor
2. Pass all quality gates before committing:
   - cargo test (ALL tests pass)
   - cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings (ZERO warnings)
   - cargo fmt --all (formatted)
   - markdown-checker -f "**/*.md" (if docs changed)
   - sw-checklist (project standards)
3. Update relevant docs if behavior changed
4. Use /mw-cp for checkpoint process
5. Push immediately after commit
6. Steps that touch the web UI must rebuild pages/ via scripts/build-pages.sh

## Goal

Add an `svg(data, type)` built-in function to MLPL so users can
visualize arrays as inline SVG diagrams in the browser REPL.
This unlocks ~10 ML demos from the learning-series blog (decision
boundary, loss curve, k-means clusters, PCA scatter, attention
heatmap, catastrophic forgetting demo, etc.).

The browser REPL detects an SVG result and renders it inline
instead of as text.

Deployed at: https://sw-ml-study.github.io/sw-mlpl/

## What already exists

- mlpl-array: DenseArray (rank 0/1/2/3+), reductions, matmul, transpose
- mlpl-runtime: 23 built-in functions
- mlpl-eval: AST evaluator
- mlpl-parser: full v1 syntax (literals, arrays, calls, repeat)
- mlpl-repl + mlpl-web: working REPLs
- Display formatting: arrays render as text grids

## Design

### svg() built-in signature

```
svg(data, type_name)            # 2-arg: data + diagram type string
svg(data, type_name, opts)      # 3-arg variant: optional config
```

`type_name` is a string (we'll need string literals or use a
sentinel approach since MLPL has no strings yet -- see decision
below).

### Diagram types (priority order)

1. **scatter** -- 2D points from an Nx2 matrix. Each row is (x, y).
2. **line** -- a vector becomes a polyline; an Nx2 matrix becomes
   (x, y) points connected by lines.
3. **bar** -- a vector becomes a bar chart with one bar per element.
4. **heatmap** -- an MxN matrix becomes a colored grid.
5. **decision_boundary** -- helper that takes a precomputed grid
   of classifier outputs (2D matrix) and renders as a heatmap with
   contour overlay.

These five cover most of the demos in the blog series.

### Output format

`svg()` returns a `DenseArray` carrying the SVG string... but
MLPL has no string type. **Decision needed.**

### CRITICAL DECISION: strings in MLPL

MLPL is array-only. There are no string values. To make svg()
work we need ONE of:

A. Add a String value type to DenseArray (invasive)
B. Add a separate Value enum at the eval layer (Number | String | Array)
   that wraps DenseArray (large refactor across crates)
C. Store strings in a side-channel: svg() returns a special "tagged"
   array (e.g., a 1-d array whose data is a slot id), and the REPL
   looks up the actual SVG string from a session-scoped registry.
   The MLPL eval layer never sees the string. (least invasive)
D. Add a new top-level Value type only in mlpl-eval, leaving
   mlpl-array unchanged. svg() returns a Value::Svg(String), and
   the evaluator's return type becomes Value instead of DenseArray.

**Recommendation: D.** It is the cleanest long-term path because
strings will be needed for LLM client work later (v0.4). It's
mid-sized (touches eval, runtime dispatch, REPL display) but
contained and reversible.

For type_name parameter: introduce string literals to the parser
("scatter") at the same time. Single-quoted or double-quoted.

This means the saga has a foundational first step: introduce a
Value type and string literals. Without this, svg() can't have a
type-name argument.

## Phases

### Phase 1: Foundation (strings + Value type)
- Add Value type to mlpl-eval
- Parser: string literals
- All existing tests still pass (Value::Array(DenseArray) wraps
  the existing return path)

### Phase 2: SVG built-in
- svg() function with scatter type
- Add line, bar, heatmap, decision_boundary types

### Phase 3: REPL integration
- CLI REPL prints raw SVG (or saves to file)
- Browser REPL detects SVG strings and renders inline

### Phase 4: Demos
- Update logistic regression demo with loss-curve viz
- Add k-means demo with cluster viz
- Add attention heatmap demo
- Update tutorial lesson(s) to introduce viz

## Success criteria

- svg([...], "scatter") returns a string starting with <svg
- Browser REPL displays scatter, line, bar, heatmap inline
- Logistic regression demo shows a loss curve below the accuracy
- All tests pass, all quality gates green
- Tutorial has at least one viz lesson

heatmap, catastrophic forgetting demo, etc.).

The browser REPL detects an SVG result and renders it inline
instead of as text.

Deployed at: https://sw-ml-study.github.io/sw-mlpl/

## What already exists

- mlpl-array: DenseArray (rank 0/1/2/3+), reductions, matmul, transpose
- mlpl-runtime: 23 built-in functions
- mlpl-eval: AST evaluator
- mlpl-parser: full v1 syntax (literals, arrays, calls, repeat)
- mlpl-repl + mlpl-web: working REPLs
- Display formatting: arrays render as text grids

## Design

### svg() built-in signature

```
svg(data, type_name)            # 2-arg: data + diagram type string
svg(data, type_name, opts)      # 3-arg variant: optional config
```

`type_name` is a string (we'll need string literals or use a
sentinel approach since MLPL has no strings yet -- see decision
below).

### Diagram types (priority order)

1. **scatter** -- 2D points from an Nx2 matrix. Each row is (x, y).
2. **line** -- a vector becomes a polyline; an Nx2 matrix becomes
   (x, y) points connected by lines.
3. **bar** -- a vector becomes a bar chart with one bar per element.
4. **heatmap** -- an MxN matrix becomes a colored grid.
5. **decision_boundary** -- helper that takes a precomputed grid
   of classifier outputs (2D matrix) and renders as a heatmap with
   contour overlay.

These five cover most of the demos in the blog series.

### Output format

`svg()` returns a `DenseArray` carrying the SVG string... but
MLPL has no string type. **Decision needed.**

### CRITICAL DECISION: strings in MLPL

MLPL is array-only. There are no string values. To make svg()
work we need ONE of:

A. Add a String value type to DenseArray (invasive)
B. Add a separate Value enum at the eval layer (Number | String | Array)
   that wraps DenseArray (large refactor across crates)
C. Store strings in a side-channel: svg() returns a special "tagged"
   array (e.g., a 1-d array whose data is a slot id), and the REPL
   looks up the actual SVG string from a session-scoped registry.
   The MLPL eval layer never sees the string. (least invasive)
D. Add a new top-level Value type only in mlpl-eval, leaving
   mlpl-array unchanged. svg() returns a Value::Svg(String), and
   the evaluator's return type becomes Value instead of DenseArray.

**Recommendation: D.** It is the cleanest long-term path because
strings will be needed for LLM client work later (v0.4). It's
mid-sized (touches eval, runtime dispatch, REPL display) but
contained and reversible.

For type_name parameter: introduce string literals to the parser
("scatter") at the same time. Single-quoted or double-quoted.

This means the saga has a foundational first step: introduce a
Value type and string literals. Without this, svg() can't have a
type-name argument.

## Phases

### Phase 1: Foundation (strings + Value type)
- Add Value type to mlpl-eval
- Parser: string literals
- All existing tests still pass (Value::Array(DenseArray) wraps
  the existing return path)

### Phase 2: SVG built-in
- svg() function with scatter type
- Add line, bar, heatmap, decision_boundary types

### Phase 3: REPL integration
- CLI REPL prints raw SVG (or saves to file)
- Browser REPL detects SVG strings and renders inline

### Phase 4: Demos
- Update logistic regression demo with loss-curve viz
- Add k-means demo with cluster viz
- Add attention heatmap demo
- Update tutorial lesson(s) to introduce viz

## Success criteria

- svg([...], "scatter") returns a string starting with <svg
- Browser REPL displays scatter, line, bar, heatmap inline
- Logistic regression demo shows a loss curve below the accuracy
- All tests pass, all quality gates green
- Tutorial has at least one viz lesson

input.command:
agentrail add --slug 001-string-literals --task-type production --prompt "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add string literals to the parser and a Value type to the evaluator.

This is the foundation step for svg() and for future work with
LLM clients that need string arguments.

1. Parser: lex string literals
   - Double-quoted: "hello world"
   - Escape sequences: \" \\ \n
   - New TokenKind::StrLit(String)
   - New Expr::StrLit(String, Span)
   - Tests for lex + parse of various string literals

2. Evaluator: introduce Value enum in mlpl-eval
   - Value::Array(DenseArray)
   - Value::Str(String)
   - Change eval_program return type to Result<Value, EvalError>
   - Most existing call sites just wrap/unwrap Value::Array
   - Built-ins still take Vec<DenseArray> and return DenseArray
     for now (no built-in needs strings yet -- svg() comes next step)
   - Update all existing tests to wrap expected results in Value::Array

3. Display: impl Display for Value
   - Array: existing display
   - Str: just the contents (no quotes)

4. REPL (CLI + web): adapt to new Value return type
   - Print Value::Array as before
   - Print Value::Str as raw content

5. Quality gates pass

Allowed: crates/mlpl-parser, crates/mlpl-eval, apps/mlpl-repl,
crates/mlpl-wasm, apps/mlpl-web
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.content: Advanced to step 001-001-string-literals.

toolUseResult.stdout: Advanced to step 001-001-string-literals.

input.command:
agentrail add --slug 002-svg-builtin-scatter --task-type production --prompt "$(cat <<'EOF'
Implement the svg() built-in with the "scatter" diagram type.

1. Create crates/mlpl-viz/src with an svg module
   - fn render_scatter(data: &DenseArray, opts: SvgOpts) -> String
   - Input: Nx2 matrix of (x, y) points
   - Output: complete <svg>...</svg> string
   - Auto-scale axes to data range
   - White background, dark dots, light grid
   - 400x300 default size

2. Wire svg() built-in in mlpl-runtime (or mlpl-eval since
   it returns a Value::Str now):
   - svg(data, type_name) where type_name is a Str arg
   - Match on type_name: "scatter" -> render_scatter(...)
   - Other types return error: "unknown svg type"
   - Returns Value::Str(svg_string)

3. Tests:
   - svg([[0,0],[1,1],[2,4]], "scatter") returns string starting "<svg"
   - String contains expected SVG element count
   - Empty data renders an empty plot
   - Wrong shape (vector instead of matrix) returns clean error
   - Unknown type returns clean error

4. Keep mlpl-viz placeholder doc removed; make this its first
   real feature.

Allowed: crates/mlpl-viz, crates/mlpl-runtime, crates/mlpl-eval
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.content: Added step 002-002-svg-builtin-scatter.

toolUseResult.stdout: Added step 002-002-svg-builtin-scatter.

input.command:
agentrail add --slug 003-svg-line-bar --task-type production --prompt "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add "line" and "bar" diagram types to svg().

1. render_line(data, opts) -> String
   - Vector input: x = 0..N-1, y = element values (loss curves)
   - Nx2 matrix input: (x, y) points connected by polyline
   - Auto-scale axes
   - Smooth polyline, with optional dot markers

2. render_bar(data, opts) -> String
   - Vector input: one bar per element
   - Auto-scale Y axis
   - Equal-width bars with small gaps

3. Wire into svg() dispatch:
   - "line" -> render_line
   - "bar" -> render_bar

4. Tests:
   - svg([0.5, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1], "line") -- loss curve
   - svg([3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6], "bar") -- histogram
   - Both render valid SVG strings
   - Both auto-scale correctly

Allowed: crates/mlpl-viz, crates/mlpl-runtime, crates/mlpl-eval
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.content: Added step 003-003-svg-line-bar.

toolUseResult.stdout: Added step 003-003-svg-line-bar.

input.command:
agentrail add --slug 004-svg-heatmap --task-type production --prompt "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add "heatmap" diagram type for visualizing matrices.

1. render_heatmap(data, opts) -> String
   - Input: MxN matrix
   - Each cell becomes a colored rectangle
   - Color scale: viridis-like (dark purple -> teal -> yellow)
   - Auto-scale color range to min/max of data
   - Optional cell values overlaid for small matrices
   - Optional row/column labels

2. Color helper functions
   - value_to_rgb(v: f64, min: f64, max: f64) -> (u8, u8, u8)
   - Stay simple: linear interpolation across 3 control points
     for a viridis-like ramp

3. Wire into svg() dispatch: "heatmap" -> render_heatmap

4. Tests:
   - svg(reshape(iota(12), [3, 4]), "heatmap") returns valid SVG
   - svg with all-equal values doesn't NaN
   - 1x1 matrix works
   - Large matrix (100x100) renders without choking

Allowed: crates/mlpl-viz, crates/mlpl-runtime, crates/mlpl-eval
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.content: Added step 004-004-svg-heatmap.

input.command:
agentrail add --slug 005-repl-inline-svg --task-type production --prompt "$(cat <<'EOF'
Make both REPLs render SVG output specially.

1. Browser REPL (apps/mlpl-web):
   - In render_entry, detect if entry.output starts with "<svg"
   - If yes: use Yew's Html::from_html_unchecked or
     gloo dangerously_set_inner_html equivalent
     to render the SVG inline (not as text)
   - Add CSS so the SVG sits in a bordered card with padding
   - If the output is plain text, render as before in <pre>

2. CLI REPL (apps/mlpl-repl):
   - If output starts with "<svg", offer two behaviors:
     a. Print "[svg: N bytes]" placeholder
     b. With --svg-out flag, write each SVG to a numbered file
        in cwd (svg-001.svg, svg-002.svg, ...)
   - Default: placeholder

3. Tests:
   - Browser: manual verification (build + serve)
   - CLI: integration test that writes a file with --svg-out

4. Update apps/mlpl-web pages/ via build-pages.sh

Allowed: apps/mlpl-web, apps/mlpl-repl, crates/mlpl-eval
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.content: Added step 005-005-repl-inline-svg.

input.command:
agentrail add --slug 006-loss-curve-demo --task-type production --prompt "$(cat <<'EOF'
Update the logistic regression demo and tutorial to show a
loss curve via svg().

1. Logistic regression demo (demos/logistic_regression.mlpl):
   - During training, append the loss to a vector each iteration
   - After training, render svg(losses, "line")
   - Also render svg(predictions, "bar") to compare to expected

2. Add a new demo: demos/loss_curve.mlpl
   - Simple 1D linear regression with svg loss-curve viz
   - Demonstrates the pattern in isolation

3. Update web demo selector (apps/mlpl-web/src/demos.rs):
   - Add "Loss Curve" demo
   - Update Logistic Regression demo to include svg() lines

4. Update interactive tutorial:
   - Add Lesson 11 "Visualizing Data": svg() with scatter,
     line, bar, heatmap examples
   - Update Lesson 10 (Logistic Regression) to include the
     loss curve visualization

5. Quality gates pass, pages rebuilt

Allowed: demos/, apps/mlpl-web/src/, docs/

input.command:
agentrail add --slug 007-decision-boundary --task-type production --prompt "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add the "decision_boundary" diagram type and a classifier demo.

1. Add helper function to generate a 2D grid of evaluation points
   - grid(x_min, x_max, y_min, y_max, n) -> Nx2 matrix
   - Wire as built-in: grid(bounds, n) where bounds is [xmin,xmax,ymin,ymax]

2. render_decision_boundary in mlpl-viz:
   - Inputs: grid points (Nx2), classifier outputs at each grid point
     (vector of length N), and original training data with labels
   - Render heatmap of classifier outputs over the 2D space
   - Overlay scatter of training points colored by true label
   - Returns full SVG

3. Wire as svg(grid_outputs, "decision_boundary", training_data)
   - The 3-arg variant of svg() (optional opts is a third positional)

4. Demo: train softmax/logistic on 2D toy dataset (XOR or two
   moons via simple geometry), then render decision boundary

5. Add demo to web demo selector

Note: this step depends on softmax/argmax being available. If
they aren't built-in yet, scope this step to logistic regression
on a linearly separable dataset (e.g., AND gate extended to a
2D point cloud) and defer multi-class to later.

Allowed: crates/mlpl-viz, crates/mlpl-runtime, crates/mlpl-eval,
demos/, apps/mlpl-web/src/
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.text:
**mlpl-svg-viz saga** is set up with 8 steps:

| # | Step | Type |
|---|------|------|
| 1 | **String literals + Value type** — foundation, also unblocks LLM client work | production |
| 2 | **svg() with scatter** — first diagram type | production |
| 3 | **line + bar types** — loss curves and histograms | production |
| 4 | **heatmap type** — viridis-like color ramp | production |
| 5 | **REPL inline SVG** — browser renders inline, CLI placeholder/--svg-out | production |
| 6 | **Loss curve demo + tutorial Lesson 11** — visualization tutorial | production |
| 7 | **decision_boundary + grid()** — classifier visualization | production |
| 8 | **Docs + v0.3.0-viz tag** | validation |

Key design decision baked into step 001: introduce a `Value::{Array, Str}` enum at the eval layer rather than poisoning `DenseArray` with strings. This is invasive but pays for itself when the LLM client lands.

Run `go` to begin step 001.

content.content:
- Value::Array(DenseArray)
   - Value::Str(String)
   - Change eval_program return type to Result<Value, EvalError>
   - Most existing call sites just wrap/unwrap Value::Array
   - Built-ins still take Vec<DenseArray> and return DenseArray
     for now (no built-in needs strings yet -- svg() comes next step)
   - Update all existing tests to wrap expected results in Value::Array

3. Display: impl Display for Value
   - Array: existing display
   - Str: just the contents (no quotes)

4. REPL (CLI + web): adapt to new Value return type
   - Print Value::Array as before
   - Print Value::Str as raw content

content.content:
1	//! AST node types for MLPL.
2	//!
3	//! These are parser-owned syntax nodes. They do NOT depend on mlpl-array.
4	
5	use mlpl_core::Span;
6	
7	/// Binary operator kind.
8	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
9	pub enum BinOpKind {
10	    /// `+`
11	    Add,
12	    /// `-`
13	    Sub,
14	    /// `*`
15	    Mul,
16	    /// `/`
17	    Div,
18	}
19	
20	/// An expression in the MLPL AST.
21	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
22	pub enum Expr {
23	    /// Integer literal.
24	    IntLit(i64, Span),
25	    /// Float literal.
26	    FloatLit(f64, Span),
27	    /// Identifier reference.
28	    Ident(String, Span),
29	    /// Array literal: `[expr, expr, ...]`
30	    ArrayLit(Vec<Expr>, Span),
31	    /// Binary operation: `lhs op rhs`
32	    BinOp {
33	        /// The operator.
34	        op: BinOpKind,
35	        /// Left-hand side.
36	        lhs: Box<Expr>,
37	        /// Right-hand side.
38	        rhs: Box<Expr>,
39	        /// Span covering the full expression.
40	        span: Span,
41	    },
42	    /// Function call: `name(args...)`
43	    FnCall {
44	        /// Function name.
45	        name: String,
46	        /// Arguments.
47	        args: Vec<Expr>,
48	        /// Span covering name through closing paren.
49	        span: Span,
50	    },
51	    /// Unary negation: `-expr`
52	    UnaryNeg {
53	        /// The operand.
54	        operand: Box<Expr>,
55	        /// Span covering the minus through the operand.
56	        span: Span,
57	    },
58	    /// Assignment: `name = value`
59	    Assign {
60	        /// Variable name.
61	        name: String,
62	        /// Value expression.
63	        value: Box<Expr>,
64	        /// Span covering name through value.
65	        span: Span,
66	    },
67	    /// Repeat loop: `repeat <count> { body }`
68	    Repeat {
69	        /// Number of iterations.
70	        count: Box<Expr>,
71	        /// Body statements.
72	        body: Vec<Expr>,
73	        /// Span covering keyword through closing brace.
74	        span: Span,
75	    },
76	}
77	
78	impl Expr {
79	    /// Return the source span for this expression.
80	    #[must_use]
81	    pub fn span(&self) -> Span {
82	        match self {
83	            Self::IntLit(_, s)
84	            | Self::FloatLit(_, s)
85	            | Self::Ident(_, s)
86	            | Self::ArrayLit(_, s)
87	            | Self::BinOp { span: s, .. }
88	            | Self::UnaryNeg { span: s, .. }
89	            | Self::FnCall { span: s, .. }
90	            | Self::Assign { span: s, .. }
91	            | Self::Repeat { span: s, .. } => *s,
92	        }
93	    }
94	}
95

content.text:
# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`

If  was provided, incorporate it as context for the message.

Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

---

## Phase 5 — Push

1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`

If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.

---

## Phase 6 — Checkpoint Summary

Report a brief summary:
- ✅ Checks passed
- 📝 Docs updated (list files)
- 🔖 Commit hash and message
- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

Flag any items that were skipped or need follow-up.
```

---

## Usage
```
/mw-cp
/mw-cp adds OAuth token refresh with retry logic

content.text:
Changes:
- **mlpl-parser**: `TokenKind::StrLit`, `Expr::StrLit`, `lex_string` helper in `lex_util.rs` (handles `\"`, `\\`, `\n`, `\t`, `\r`), parser primary expression handles StrLit, `describe_kind` formats it
- **mlpl-eval**: new `Value::{Array, Str}` enum + `value.rs`, new `eval_ops.rs` module (binop/fncall/array_lit), `eval.rs` slimmed down with `eval_program_value` returning Value, `EvalError::ExpectedArray` for str-where-array-expected
- **mlpl-wasm**: uses `eval_program_value` so strings flow through to the REPL
- **mlpl-repl**: uses `eval_program_value`
- **Tests**: `string_lit_tests.rs` (11 tests for lex/parse), `value_string_tests.rs` (5 tests for eval), 2 new wasm tests

content.content: (Bash completed with no output)

content.text: ## Phase 4 — Commit

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat: add string literals and Value type for upcoming svg() built-in

Foundation for mlpl-svg-viz saga and future LLM-client work that
needs string-typed arguments. The language gains:

Parser:
- TokenKind::StrLit and Expr::StrLit
- Double-quoted strings with escapes: \" \\ \n \t \r
- lex_string helper in lex_util to keep lexer.rs under the
  function-count limit
- describe_kind formats string tokens cleanly

Evaluator:
- New Value enum: Value::Array(DenseArray) | Value::Str(String)
- eval_program_value() returns Value (for callers that handle strings)
- eval_program() still returns DenseArray (errors with ExpectedArray
  if final value is a string) so existing callers and tests are
  unchanged
- eval_program_traced() unchanged signature; tracing still array-only
- EvalError::ExpectedArray for "string where array required"
- eval_ops module split out (binop/fncall/array_lit) to keep
  eval.rs under the module function-count limit

Consumers:
- mlpl-wasm WasmSession::eval and eval_line use eval_program_value
- mlpl-repl uses eval_program_value
- Browser and CLI REPLs now display string results as raw text

Tests:
- 11 new parser tests covering lex + parse of various strings
- 5 new eval tests for Value (string return, error on str+arith,
  last-statement decides type)
- 2 new WasmSession tests for string round-trip and error path

Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1

# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`

If  was provided, incorporate it as context for the message.

Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

---

## Phase 5 — Push

1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`

If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.

---

## Phase 6 — Checkpoint Summary

Report a brief summary:
- ✅ Checks passed
- 📝 Docs updated (list files)
- 🔖 Commit hash and message
- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

Flag any items that were skipped or need follow-up.
```

---

## Usage
```
/mw-cp
/mw-cp adds OAuth token refresh with retry logic

Changes:
- **mlpl-viz** gains a real implementation: `svg::render(data, type)` dispatcher and `render_scatter(data)` for Nx2 matrices, with `VizError` (InvalidShape, UnknownType). Catppuccin Mocha colored dots, axes, auto-scaled bounds.
- **mlpl-eval**: depends on mlpl-viz, new `EvalError::ExpectedString`, `BadArity`, `VizError` variants. `eval_expr` special-cases `FnCall { name: "svg", .. }` and routes to `eval_svg` which evaluates first arg as Array, second as Str, calls viz dispatcher.
- **Tests**: 7 new viz unit tests, 2 new wasm end-to-end tests for svg via WasmSession.

git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(mlpl-viz): implement svg() built-in with scatter type

mlpl-viz becomes a real crate (was placeholder). Adds:

mlpl-viz:
- svg::render(data, type_name) dispatcher
- svg::render_scatter(Nx2 matrix) returning a complete <svg> string
  with Catppuccin Mocha colors, axes, auto-scaled bounds, and one
  <circle> per row
- VizError: InvalidShape and UnknownType
- 7 unit tests covering happy path, empty data, single point,

content.text:
# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`

If  was provided, incorporate it as context for the message.

Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

---

## Phase 5 — Push

1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`

If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.

---

## Phase 6 — Checkpoint Summary

Report a brief summary:
- ✅ Checks passed
- 📝 Docs updated (list files)
- 🔖 Commit hash and message
- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

Flag any items that were skipped or need follow-up.
```

---

## Usage
```
/mw-cp
/mw-cp adds OAuth token refresh with retry logic
## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(mlpl-viz): add line and bar svg diagram types

Reorganize mlpl-viz/src/svg.rs into svg/ directory with one module
per diagram type (helpers, scatter, line, bar). Required to keep
each module under the function-count limit.

New diagrams:
- render_line: accepts a vector (x = 0..N-1) for loss curves, or
  an Nx2 matrix for explicit (x, y) points. Emits <polyline> with
  the Catppuccin blue stroke. Auto-scales bounds.
- render_bar: vector input only, one <rect> per element. Y range
  always includes zero so positive values draw upward from a
  baseline. Equal-width bars with 20% gaps, Catppuccin green fill.

Dispatcher updated:
  svg(data, "line")    -> polyline plot
  svg(data, "bar")     -> bar chart
  svg(data, "scatter") -> existing scatter plot

10 new tests cover vector + matrix line input, polyline element
count, empty data, constant values (no NaN), bar element count,
bar empty case, shape rejection (matrix to bar, 3D to line), and
end-to-end dispatch through render().

Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.text: I'll add a new step for analysis helpers. It'll come after the existing validation step (008), so I'll plan it as the saga's wrap-up extension.

input.command:
agentrail add --slug 009-analysis-helpers --task-type production --prompt "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add high-level analysis/visualization helper functions that
generate large/complex SVG diagrams from data, analyses of
data, or views over data.

Motivation: svg(data, type) is a low-level primitive. Real
demos need higher-level helpers that compute the right view
of the data and produce a publication-quality diagram.

1. Add new built-ins (each returns Value::Str via the same
   FnCall special-case path used by svg()):

   - hist(data, bins) -> svg
       Compute a histogram from a vector and render as a bar
       chart with bin edges. `bins` is an integer count.

   - scatter_labeled(points, labels) -> svg
       Nx2 points + length-N labels (cluster ids). Each label
       gets a distinct color from a palette.

   - loss_curve(losses) -> svg
       Vector of loss values, rendered as a line plot with
       Y-axis log option, axis ticks, and a "loss" label.

   - confusion_matrix(predicted, actual) -> svg
       Two vectors of class ids. Compute the confusion matrix
       and render as a heatmap with cell value overlays.

   - boundary_2d(grid_outputs, grid_dims, points, labels) -> svg
       Render a 2D classifier decision boundary by treating
       grid_outputs as a heatmap with training points overlaid.

   - layout_grid(svgs) -> svg  (stretch goal)
       Combine multiple svg strings into a single grid layout.
       Useful for "compare runs" panels.

2. New module: crates/mlpl-viz/src/analysis/ with one file per
   helper. Each helper takes &DenseArray (and possibly other
   args) and returns Result<String, VizError>.

3. mlpl-eval: dispatch each new helper name in eval_expr the
   same way svg() is dispatched. Consider extracting a
   "value-returning builtin" registry to avoid hand-rolling
   each one.

4. Tests:
   - Unit tests in mlpl-viz for each helper
   - End-to-end wasm tests showing they return strings starting
     with <svg

5. Docs: update docs/lang-reference.md with the new helpers,
   docs/usage.md with example usage, and add a tutorial lesson
   for "Visualizing Analyses".

6. Add a demo (demos/analysis_demo.mlpl) that walks through
   training a model, computing a confusion matrix, and rendering
   loss curves + boundary in one go.

7. Quality gates pass, pages rebuilt.

Allowed: crates/mlpl-viz, crates/mlpl-eval, demos/, docs/,
apps/mlpl-web, apps/mlpl-repl
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.text:
# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`

If  was provided, incorporate it as context for the message.

Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

---

## Phase 5 — Push

1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`

If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.

---

## Phase 6 — Checkpoint Summary

Report a brief summary:
- ✅ Checks passed
- 📝 Docs updated (list files)
- 🔖 Commit hash and message
- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

Flag any items that were skipped or need follow-up.
```

---

## Usage
```
/mw-cp
/mw-cp adds OAuth token refresh with retry logic

content.text: ## Phase 4 — Commit

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(mlpl-viz): add heatmap diagram type with viridis color ramp

Adds the fourth svg() diagram type. render_heatmap takes an MxN
matrix and emits one <rect> per cell, colored by a viridis-like
ramp (dark purple -> teal -> yellow). Auto-scales the color range
to the data and clamps to [0, 1]. Constant-valued matrices map
to a single mid-ramp color (no NaN).

The viridis() helper interpolates linearly across three control
points which is good enough for the explainer visuals; a true
viridis LUT can replace it later if needed.

Dispatcher: svg(matrix, "heatmap")

Also adds saga step 009 (analysis helpers) per user request.
That step will add high-level helpers like hist, scatter_labeled,
loss_curve, confusion_matrix, boundary_2d, layout_grid that
generate complex SVGs from data or analyses.

8 new tests cover: SVG envelope, rect-per-cell count, constant
values, single cell, vector/3D rejection, dispatch routing, and
color ramp presence.

Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1

content.text:
# /mw-cp — Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process

You are executing a disciplined checkpoint commit. Follow every phase in order.
Do NOT skip any phase, even if it seems clean. Report status after each phase.

---

## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Run all of the following that apply to this project. Stop and report failures before proceeding.

1. **Format**: `cargo fmt --check` (or `rustfmt --check` on specific files)
2. **Lint**: `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. **Build**: `cargo build` (debug is fine; surface any compile errors)
4. **Tests**: `cargo test` — all tests must pass
5. **Unused deps** (if `cargo-machete` present): `cargo machete`
6. **Project-local checks**: run `just check` or `just pre-commit` if a justfile exists

If any check fails, STOP. Report what failed and ask the user how to proceed.

---

## Phase 2 — Documentation Updates

Review recent changes and update the following as appropriate:

1. **CHANGELOG.md / CHANGES.md** — add an entry under `[Unreleased]` or today's date
2. **STATUS.md** — update current state, what changed, what's in progress
3. **LEARNINGS.md** — capture anything notable: decisions made, gotchas, tradeoffs
4. **Inline docs** — ensure any new public API items have doc comments (`///`)
5. **README.md** — update if any user-facing behavior, config, or setup changed

If none of these files exist, note that and skip gracefully.

---

## Phase 3 — Staged Diff Review

Run `git diff --staged` (or `git diff HEAD` if nothing staged yet).

Summarize what is changing:
- Files modified, added, deleted
- High-level functional description of the change

Then stage all changes: `git add -A`

---

## Phase 4 — Commit

Compose a detailed commit message following Conventional Commits style:
```
<type>(<scope>): <short imperative summary>

<body — explain WHAT changed and WHY, not HOW>
<mention any tradeoffs, alternatives considered, or follow-ups needed>

Refs: <issue/ticket numbers if applicable>
Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

Types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `build`

If  was provided, incorporate it as context for the message.

Execute: `git commit -m "<message>"`

---

## Phase 5 — Push

1. Check current branch: `git branch --show-current`
2. Check remote: `git remote -v`
3. Push: `git push origin <branch>`

If the push is rejected (non-fast-forward), STOP and report — do NOT force push without explicit user confirmation.

---

## Phase 6 — Checkpoint Summary

Report a brief summary:
- ✅ Checks passed
- 📝 Docs updated (list files)
- 🔖 Commit hash and message
- 🚀 Pushed to remote/branch

Flag any items that were skipped or need follow-up.
```

---

## Usage
```
/mw-cp
/mw-cp adds OAuth token refresh with retry logic
## Phase 1 — Pre-Commit Checks

Changes:
- **mlpl-web/src/main.rs** -- `render_entry` detects SVG output and uses `Html::from_html_unchecked` to render it inline; non-SVG output stays in `<pre>`
- **index.html** -- new `.svg-output` CSS card with mantle background and border
- **mlpl-repl**: new `svg_out.rs` module with `SvgOut::handle` (writes to dir if `--svg-out <dir>` flag set, else prints `[svg: N bytes]` placeholder), `--svg-out` CLI flag, `eval_line` detects `<svg` prefix and routes to SvgOut
- **pages/** -- rebuilt with inline SVG rendering

content.text: ## Phase 4 — Commit

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat: render svg() output inline in browser, --svg-out in CLI

Browser REPL:
- render_entry detects when an output line starts with "<svg"
  and uses Html::from_html_unchecked to render the SVG inline
  inside a .svg-output card (mantle background, surface1 border).
- Non-SVG and error outputs continue to render in <pre>.

CLI REPL:
- New svg_out module with SvgOut struct.
- --svg-out <dir> CLI flag enables saving each SVG result as
  svg-001.svg, svg-002.svg, ... in <dir>.
- Without the flag, the CLI prints "[svg: N bytes -- pass
  --svg-out <dir> to save]" so the user knows something
  happened without flooding the terminal with markup.
- eval_line detects "<svg" prefix on the formatted result and
  routes to SvgOut::handle.

Smoke-tested: placeholder mode and --svg-out file writing both
work end-to-end via stdin pipe.

Pages rebuilt to deploy the inline SVG rendering.

Co-authored-by: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1
